name: Minor Triad
feel: Foundational dark chord — most garage pads and bass targets start here.
analytical_note: Root + 3 semitones + 7 semitones. The minor 3rd (vs major 3rd at 4 semitones) is what makes it sound dark/sad. Every minor key centers on this chord.

name: Major 7
feel: Lush, dreamy, slightly bittersweet. The maj7 chord sound is a defining colour of Kettama-style garage.
examples: Gbmaj7 in Kettama — It Gets Better (VI chord)
in_progressions: bVI chord in minor key (e.g. Gbmaj7 in Bb minor)
analytical_note: The major 7th sits 1 semitone below the octave. That tension against the root gives the chord its wistful quality. Kettama's Gbmaj7→Dbmaj→Bbm loop uses this as the 'lift' chord — same function as a bVI.

name: bVI (Flat-Six) Lift
feel: Bright, emotional lift within a minor key. The borrowed major chord that resolves tension.
examples: C major in Interplanetary Criminal E minor (bVI = 8 semitones up from E)
in_progressions: i → v → bVI (IC pattern: Em → Bm → C)
VI → III → i (Kettama: Gbmaj7 → Dbmaj → Bbm)
analytical_note: The bVI is a major chord built on the 6th note of the natural minor scale. In E minor the 6th note is C, so C major is the bVI. It's 'borrowed' — C major isn't diatonic to E minor (C would naturally be minor). The major quality gives the lift.

name: Bass-Implied Harmony
feel: Chord feels implied by the bass root note alone, with no full chord played. Creates ambiguity and space.
examples: BL3SS — Kisses: Dm→F→Bb→Gm bass line implies full chord progression with no chord instrument
in_progressions: i → bIII → bVI → iv (BL3SS: Dm → F → Bb → Gm)
analytical_note: When a bass note moves through D→F→Bb→G, the ear fills in Dm→Fmaj→Bbmaj→Gm even without chords being played. This is how BL3SS creates harmony — two bass tracks (organ + FM) carry all harmonic information. Powerful for minimalist productions.

name: Borrowed Major Chord
feel: Unexpected brightness on the tonic — dramatic, anthemic. The 'major in a minor key' moment.
examples: G#maj in Sammy Virji — Cops & Robbers (tonic as major, not G#m)
in_progressions: G#maj → Emaj → Bmaj (V-III-VII in G# minor with all majors)
analytical_note: In G# minor, the natural tonic chord is G#m. Sammy Virji uses G# major instead — the Picardy-third equivalent. All three stab chords are major (G#maj, Emaj, Bmaj), creating an anthemic, triumphant feel despite the minor key context. The raised 3rd (B# = C) on G#maj comes from harmonic minor.

name: Suspended Chord
feel: Unresolved, floating — neither major nor minor. Creates anticipation before resolving.
examples: Common in garage breakdowns and transitions
Used on synth pads to avoid committing to major/minor
in_progressions: Often used as passing chord: Em → Esus4 → Em
analytical_note: Replace the 3rd with a 2nd (sus2) or 4th (sus4). No 3rd = no major/minor identity. Sus chords float harmonically — they want to resolve. Often used in breakdowns to build tension before the drop.
